title: Executive Order 12999—Educational Technology: Ensuring Opportunity for All Children in the Next Century

flags.justification: A beneficial, distributive directive prioritizing schools and nonprofits for surplus federal computer equipment, not a concentration of authority.
Sec. 3(b) requires agencies to submit implementation plans within six months, coordinated through the Office of Science and Technology Policy.
Multiple related provisions (equipment transfer, teacher volunteering, definitions) all coherently serve one purpose: educational technology access.
A beneficial distribution directive, not a compliance regime against private parties.
No compliance regime exists for an incentive structure to attach to.
A standing policy directive for an ongoing beneficial purpose.

Sec. 6 states this order does not create any right or benefit enforceable at law by a party against the United States — a fifth confirmed instance of the no-private-right-of-action pattern already logged in this project (EO 12114, EO 12615, EO 12662, EO 12735).
This concern is captured under Flag 7.
A notable digital-divide and educational-access initiative from the mid-1990s 'National Information Infrastructure' policy era.
This is a benefit-distribution directive; preference categories for recipients are not exemptions from a regulatory scheme.

notes: Validation batch (negative). Structural weight: 2/12 = 16.7%. A fifth confirmed instance of the no-private-right-of-action clause, now spanning environmental review, procurement, trade adjudication, sanctions, and educational technology — a genuinely broad range of subject matter for what appears to be a standard executive-order drafting convention.
